title: Validated text input

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Validated text input; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.claim: Robust C input checks each function's return result, distinguishes token from line input, bounds every destination, and handles leftover or invalid characters deliberately.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Robust C input checks each function's return result, distinguishes token from line input, bounds every destination, and handles leftover or invalid characters deliberately.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A line is read with fgets into a bounded array, its newline is handled, and strtol validates complete numeric conversion and range. Your goal is to implement a console input loop that rejects malformed and out-of-range values safely. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Operators and precedence; pf control iteration; while, do and for; Conversions and numeric boundaries. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Robust C input checks each function's return result, distinguishes token from line input, bounds every destination, and handles leftover or invalid characters deliberately. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: scanf conversion failure can leave input unread, fgets retains a newline when space permits, EOF differs from an empty line, and locale can affect conversions. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: scanf with %s or %d always writes a usable value, so its return result and destination width can be ignored.

includes: Read bounded text, parse values and handle malformed or exhausted input.
Implement a console input loop that rejects malformed and out-of-range values safely.
excludes: scanf conversion failure can leave input unread, fgets retains a newline when space permits, EOF differs from an empty line, and locale can affect conversions.

outcomes: Implement a console input loop that rejects malformed and out-of-range values safely.

examples.title: Validated text input: defined case
examples.situation: A line is read with fgets into a bounded array, its newline is handled, and strtol validates complete numeric conversion and range.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A line is read with fgets into a bounded array, its newline is handled, and strtol validates complete numeric conversion and range.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Check the input function's return value before reading destinations and provide explicit bounds such as fgets capacity or a width-limited string conversion.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Check the input function's return value before reading destinations and provide explicit bounds such as fgets capacity or a width-limited string conversion.

examples.title: Validated text input: boundary case
examples.situation: A scanf call checks that exactly one integer conversion succeeded before using the destination object.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A scanf call checks that exactly one integer conversion succeeded before using the destination object.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: scanf conversion failure can leave input unread, fgets retains a newline when space permits, EOF differs from an empty line, and locale can affect conversions.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: scanf with %s or %d always writes a usable value, so its return result and destination width can be ignored.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement a console input loop that rejects malformed and out-of-range values safely.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: scanf with %s or %d always writes a usable value, so its return result and destination width can be ignored.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Validated text input: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
